nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; commonly found on shallow reef flats, in protected lagoons, around debris and shipwrecks. Typically 0–4 m depth though recorded to about 30 m.

decorPreferences: ample live rock with caves, crevices and overhangs sized to conceal the entire body
stable, secured rockwork (this species tends to rearrange substrate)
tight-fitting, weighted lid with no gaps (escape risk is high)

diet.notes: Has been documented in the wild eating small fishes, crustaceans (crabs, shrimp), and octopi. In aquariums it is generally fed meaty marine foods such as silversides, shrimp, squid, scallops and clams. Feeding tongs or a feeding stick are commonly advised — vision is poor and the eel may strike at hands during feeding, and bites can introduce serious bacterial infection. Most keepers feed a large meal every 3–4 days rather than daily, as the species is prone to overfeeding.

goodWith: larger semi-aggressive or aggressive reef fish (too large to be swallowed)
non-crustacean invertebrates large enough to avoid predation
avoidWith: shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs and other crustaceans (natural prey)
very passive or slow tankmates that cannot defend themselves at feeding time

summary: The White-eyed Moray (Gymnothorax thyrsoideus) is a slender Indo-Pacific moray with a grey head and pale eyes. It is generally considered one of the more manageable morays for the home aquarium, but is a determined escape artist and a confirmed predator of small fish and crustaceans.
careNotes:
The White-eyed Moray (also commonly sold as the Greyface Moray) is a relatively slender, mid-sized moray reaching about 66–73 cm in the wild though most captive specimens tend to stay closer to 40 cm. It has a speckled tan-to-beige body, a distinctly grey-purple head, and the pale white irises that give the species its common name.

Minimum tank size is one of the more disputed parameters across sources: retailer pages (LiveAquaria, BlueZoo) cite 50 US gallons (~190 L), while specialist sources such as ReefApp recommend 800 L for adults, and general hobby guidance lands at 75–120 gallons. The value used here (300 L) biases toward the larger end of typical retail guidance because adult specimens reach roughly 70 cm, need a long footprint for the body to fully extend, and are active nocturnal hunters that benefit from open swimming space at night. Larger tanks (450 L+) are generally advised for full-grown adults housed with tankmates.

A tightly-sealed, weighted lid is essential. This species has been widely reported to push loose covers aside, squeeze through small gaps, and even lift unweighted glass tops; escape incidents that result in death are well-documented across the hobby. Rockwork should be secured to the tank bottom because the eel tends to rearrange substrate and undermine free-standing structures.

Reef safety is nuanced: corals are generally not targeted, but any shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs or small fish in the system will be treated as food. It is best considered reef-safe-with-caution rather than fully reef-safe. Tankmates should be either too large to swallow or not part of its natural prey base.

The species is strongly nocturnal — it shelters in caves during daylight and hunts at night — so feeding should generally be done in the evening or with the lights dim. Vision is poor and bites at feeding time are a recognized hazard; tongs or a feeding stick are the standard precaution, and any bite wound should be cleaned promptly given the documented risk of bacterial infection from moray bites.

Temperature recommendations differ between hobby sources (72–78°F / 22–26°C) and FishBase wild-habitat data (24.9–29.3°C, mean 28.6°C); the range used here (23–27°C) is averaged across sources. Salinity ranges given by sources span 1.020–1.026 SG, also averaged here.

Lifespan in captivity is not well documented but is presumed similar to other Gymnothorax morays — commonly 10+ years with appropriate husbandry, though figures should be treated as estimates.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Gymnothorax thyrsoideus has not been reliably documented. Like most morays, it is presumed to spawn pelagically with a long leptocephalus larval stage, which makes rearing in home aquariums impractical.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria White-eyed Moray Eel page (/products/white-eyed-moray-eel) — confirmed 29 in adult size, 50 gal minimum (overridden upward per tank-size cautious rule), 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, semi-aggressive temperament, carnivore diet, reef-safe with caution. Note: page lists scientific name as 'Siderea thysoidea' (a junior synonym/misspelling); current accepted name is Gymnothorax thyrsoideus.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Gymnothorax thyrsoideus), 26 in adult size, 50 gal minimum, beginner care rating, semi-aggressive, reef-safe with caution, carnivore. Highlighted escape risk and bite-infection hazard.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), 73.2 cm max length (40 cm common), depth 0–30 m, wild temperature 24.9–29.3°C (averaged with hobby range), Indo-Pacific distribution from Christmas Island to French Polynesia, diet of crustaceans/small fishes/octopi. IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Gymnothorax thyrsoideus)
additional.notes: Confirmed nocturnal behavior, grey head with white eyes, beige speckled body, lagoon/protected-reef/wreck habitat. Max length cited as 66 cm; used as lower bound versus FishBase 73.2 cm.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked 66 cm adult size, 800 L minimum tank (high end of range — averaged downward with retailer values to 300 L per tank-size rule), SG 1.026, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe-with-caution, nocturnal, feed every 3–4 days. Confirmed escape-artist and rock-rearranging behaviors.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding reported. Presumed pelagic spawner with leptocephalus larval stage typical of morays.
